data.title: Exxon Mobil: A Solid Hold Even If Venezuela Is 'Uninvestable'
data.description:
Exxon Mobil, oil prices, and Venezuela

I last covered ExxonMobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM) 11-28-2025 with an comparative analysis against Occidental Petroleum (OXY). The analysis was prompted by the latest dividend changes from both companies and rated XOM as hold. Since then, a few material developments have evolved around both XOM stock and also the oil industry as a whole. In particular ,this article will share my thoughts on the development Venezuela. Key developments relevant to XOM are quoted below for readers unfamiliar with the background.

Seeking Alpha News (Jan 13): Exxon Mobil (XOM) has signaled continued interest in sending an assessment team to Venezuela for evaluating oil infrastructure and other assets. The report comes days after U.S. President Donald Trump reached out to executives from more than 20 U.S. energy companies in a live-streamed meeting Friday, with Exxon CEO Darren Woods saying that Venezuela is “uninvestable” without significant changes to the country's commercial frameworks, legal system, and hydrocarbon laws.

In the remainder of this section, I will A) explore how these developments could translate into sizable impacts for both XOM and the whole oil sector, and B) explain why XOM remains a solid investment idea even if Venezuela remains uninvestable (or uninvestable for XOM).

To start, I expect the developments in Venezuela to have long lasting impact on oil prices given the huge reserves and production potential in the country (more on this in the next section). As a background, oil prices have been already pressured lately due to the supply-demand dynamics. As shown in the first chart below, crude oil future prices have retreated from around ~$80 to the current level of $61 only in the past year, a decline of more than 20%. Brent crude oil prices declined even more, with a 23.5% pullback in the past year. Against this background, I see several reasons for oil prices to remain pressured at or even below $60 in 2026, as detailed below.

Factors that keep oil prices compressed

As just mentioned, I anticipate the developments in Venezuela to further compound the oil supply-demand issue due to its reserve and production potential. As shown in the chart below, Venezuela’s oil reserve is estimated to be about 300 billion barrels, even more than Saudi Arabia (about 267 billion) and far above the US (about 35 billion). Overall, Venezuela’s oil reserve accounts for about 20% of global reserve.

However, in contrast to its huge reserve, Venezuela’s oil production only accounts for less than 1% of global crude oil production currently, mostly due to years of political instability, lack of infrastructure improvements, and lack of capital and technology infusion as I see it. With the going political change in the country and the Trump administration’s involvement, I see large odds for these factors to change in the near-term. Especially given the geographical proximity and also many US oil companies existing presence in the country already (such as Chevron), I see large potential for the country’s oil production to ramp up and add to the global supply.

XOM stock: profit impacts from oil prices

Despite its integrated business model, I anticipate XOM’s bottom-line to be impacted materially by compressed oil prices. As an example, the next chart displays my back-of-the-envelope estimation of such impact. This estimation is based on a few assumptions: A) a daily production rate of 3.0M barrels per day based on an average of XOM’s recent throughput capacity, B) a break-even production price of $45 per barrel, and B) a share count that remains constant at its current level. Under the above parameters, the end result is that I see an EPS impact of about $0.25 per share for every $1 of oil price decline.

Based on the above estimated EPS impacts, the next table shows my projected share price changes for XOM. At the current oil price and share prices (of around $128), XOM’s FWD EPS estimate is around $6.95 and its current FWD P/E multiples are around 18.2x. Looking ahead, the most likely combinations I see are a P/E ratio of around 15x (its historical median is around 16x in the past 9 years) and an oil price in the range of $60 to $65. Under these combinations, the projected share prices fall in the range of $104 to $124 as highlighted in the chart. With an FWD dividend rate of $4.12 per share, I see a quite balanced return/risk curve as such.

Other risks and final thoughts

A few other downside risks are worth mentioning besides XOM’s uncertainty participation in Venezuela. To start, XOM shares are currently priced at a sizable premium either by its own historical norm or relative to the sector median. As you can tell from the following chart, its current TTM P/E is about 18.2x, about 1/3 above the sector median of 13.67x and more than 45% above its own 5-year average of 12.48x. Expanding the timeframe a bit, the historical median P/E is ~16x in the past 9 years as just mentioned. In terms of business fundamentals, even if XOM becomes a meaningful participation in Venezuela, there are a series of production issues the company has to resolve, which can require sustained CAPEX investments from XOM for years to come.

On the upside, there are always a few factors that can support higher oil prices and better profitability for XOM. As mentioned in a recent article, per the latest EIA data, the current status of the U.S.’s strategic petroleum reserve (“SPR”) is among the lowest levels since the 1980s and the Trump administration has been gradually refilling the SPR. Also, the OPEC+ countries have announced plans to pause production hikes to stabilize oil prices (see more details from the report and chart below). Finally, if XOM does become a meaningful participant in Venezuela, the participation could meaningfully boost XOM’s throughput and also potentially lower its break-even production prices. Furthermore, Venezuelan crude is a high-sulfur heavy crude oil, which happens to match the processing capacity of many of XOM’s refineries in my assessment and further brightens the upside.

Reuters report: “Global economic activity is expected to maintain its strong performance in both 2026 and 2027," OPEC said in the report. The OPEC+ group comprising OPEC nations, plus Russia and other allies, began raising oil output last year after years of output cuts, and plans to pause production hikes in the first quarter of 2026, amid widespread predictions of oversupply.

To close, this follow-up article on XOM is triggered mostly by the ongoing uncertainties in Venezuela. These uncertainties can both impact global oil price and also XOM’s specific operations. After examining the potential impacts, I see a well-balanced return/risk curve for XOM shares under current conditions and reiterate my hold rating.

data.title: Banner Corporation: As Earnings Near, I Remain Bullish
data.description:
Over the last few years now, I've gotten more interested in the financial sector. It was, for much of my investment career, an area that I did not tread in often. But it was actually the banking crisis of 2022 that led me to finally take the dive and see what all was going on deep down. It has been a fascinating journey since then, and many of the calls that I have made have played out well.

One decent example of this can be seen by looking at Banner Corporation (BANR), a bank with a market capitalization as of this writing of $2.19 billion. Although the stock is down 3.2% since I reaffirmed it as a “Buy” in September 2025, which is far worse than the 4.8% increase that the S&P 500 has experienced, since I originally rated it a “Buy” in September 2023, shares are up 58.6%. By comparison, the market is up a slightly more modest but still impressive 54.2%.

Even though the stock has fallen in recent months, I remain a believer in it and its potential. To be clear, I do think the easy money has been made. But I was not ready late last year to downgrade it. And looking at the data again, I feel similarly. In fact, with earnings around the corner, with management expected to announce results after the market closes on Jan. 21, I think that there is the opportunity for some more upside. This is because, in addition to improvements that management has made over time, the business is also expected to enjoy rising revenue and profits. So unless something else changes, I believe that maintaining a soft “Buy” rating is the right choice.

Not ready to stop banking on Banner

Before we get into the estimates that analysts have for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, I think it would be helpful to check out how Banner Corporation has performed as of late. The most recent quarter for which we have data available is the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Management reported deposits during that time of $14.02 billion. That's up nicely from the $13.53 billion the company had in the second quarter, and it compares favorably to the $13.51 billion the business ended the final quarter of 2024 with.

Usually when I see this much of a move higher in such a short window of time, I look at whether a surge in brokered deposits occurred. This is not necessarily a bad thing, but it helps to understand whether growth has been organic or caused by something else. After all, brokered deposits are high-cost deposits borrowed from other financial institutions. According to management, however, the company only had $50 million worth of brokered deposits during the most recent quarter. That's a dip from the $50.3 million reported the previous year. In essence, these are statistically insignificant. The real growth for the company came from regular savings accounts, which grew from $3.48 billion to $3.71 billion, and interest-bearing checking accounts that expanded from $2.39 billion to $2.73 billion. Overall, this appears healthy.

As deposits for the company increased, the value of loans also grew. The firm went from $11.20 billion worth at the end of 2024 to $11.54 billion worth in the third quarter of last year. The most significant exposure that the institution has when it comes to loans would be the $1.65 billion in investment properties that fall under the commercial real estate category. However, the company also has significant exposure when it comes to other areas as well, like owner-occupied real estate, small-balance commercial real estate, commercial business loans, small business scored loans, and one- to four-family residential properties. Each of these is in excess of $1 billion.

Other aspects of the balance sheet should not be ignored. According to management, the firm ended the most recent quarter with $2.99 billion of deposits. That is actually down from the $3.11 billion reported the previous year. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents went from $501.9 million to $672.9 million. And debt went from $563 million down to $296.8 million. This all paints an interesting picture. What this shows is that management has decided to bolster its cash position while reducing securities and growing its loan balances slower so that it can pay off debt. This is almost certainly because of a desire to boost profit margins. And as you will see in a moment, that is precisely what we have experienced.

When it comes to the income statement, things are going swimmingly well. The third quarter of 2025 saw net interest income for the company of $147.3 million. That's up from $134 million reported a year earlier. An increase in the size of the company's balance sheet contributed to this. But there was another driver as well. As interest rates are falling, the amount that banks have to pay depositors declines. This has permitted an increase in net interest margin from 3.72% to 3.98%. To think that the picture would have been even better had it not been for the fact that the provision for credit losses for the company grew from $1.7 million to $2.7 million. But I digress.

Non-interest income has also been growing. It expanded from $18.1 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $20.7 million in the third quarter of 2025. This was primarily thanks to an increase in miscellaneous income from $1.7 million to $3.2 million, followed by a growth in BOLI income from $2.4 million to $2.7 million. For context, BOLI is what management refers to as bank-owned life insurance. Regardless of the reason, this helped to push net profits up to $53.5 million compared to the $45.2 million reported the previous year. And the $144.1 million generated in profits during the first nine months of 2025 exceeded the $122.5 million reported a year earlier.

Assuming that the final quarter of 2025 looks a lot like the rest of the year has so far, we are looking at a company that's trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 11.1. This is, unfortunately, above the 10 maximum that I typically prefer. But as the chart above illustrates, Banner Corporation is cheaper than five other banks that I compared it to. In the chart below, you can see a similar ranking when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In both of these instances, only one of the institutions that I looked at ended up being cheaper than it is.

This relative valuation certainly has played a role in my decision to remain bullish. But that on its own would not be sufficient. I am also drawn by the fact that asset quality is high. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.30%. I typically prefer this to be 1% or greater. This also puts the institution above four of the five banks that I compared it to. And in the subsequent chart, you can see a return on equity rating of 11.33%. My preference is for this to be 10% or greater. So the firm does pass on that. On top of this, it is superior to these other five banks that I compared it to. So asset quality, on the whole, looks great.

We should also touch, even if only briefly, on credit quality metrics. And here is another area where Banner Corporation shines. The company ended the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year with only 0.27% of its assets classified as non-performing. This is admittedly up from the 0.24% that we saw at the end of the previous fiscal year. But it's comfortably below the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. Similarly, only 0.33% of its loans were classified as being non-accrual during that time. That's barely any different from the 0.32% reported at the end of 2024. And, once again, it is below the 0.50% maximum I like to look at.

At the start of this article, I did mention that management will be reporting financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on Jan. 2. The good news is that, leading up to that point, analysts seem quite bullish. The firm is expected to report revenue of $173.2 million. This is synonymous with the sum of net interest income without factoring in the provision for credit losses and non-interest income. During the final quarter of 2024, revenue defined this way came in at $160.5 million. So analysts expect a rather nice increase year over year. With that, earnings per share are expected to climb from $1.34 to $1.45. This would mean an increase in net profits from $46.4 million to $50.3 million year over year.

Takeaway

As things stand, it is my belief that Banner Corporation is an attractive prospect. It's not as cheap as it once was, and the upside is limited because of that. But asset quality is high, and credit quality metrics are solid. The balance sheet and the income statement continue to show year-over-year progress. And on top of this, the company is cheap relative to other similar firms. When you add all of this together, I do believe that a soft "Buy" rating is appropriate.

data.title: MoneyShow's Best Investment Ideas For 2026: Part 4
data.description:
MoneyShow’s top contributing analysts, strategists, and newsletter editors share their investment recommendations for the year ahead. See Part 3 here.

Eli Lilly & Co.

By Mike Cintolo, Chief Investment Strategist at Cabot Wealth Network

Eli Lilly & Co. (LLY) needs little introduction, as it’s probably the most popular drug stock in the world right now thanks to Mounjaro (diabetes) and Zepbound (weight loss), which are the same drug selling well under both indications. But we still think the stock can surprise on the upside going forward.

In Q3 2025, Mounjaro’s sales lifted 109% and Zepbound’s boomed 185%. Combined, they made up 57% of total revenue, which itself grew 54% from the year before.

Of course, the success of these drugs has been well known for a while, and in the market, that means a lot has been discounted. When combined with some regulatory fears (the Trump Administration has made it a point to get drug prices down, which Lilly has agreed to), it’s a big reason why the stock actually lost ground from July 2024 through October 2025.

But now the stock is back in gear, surging to new all-time highs for a few reasons. The first is that the weight loss franchise should continue to take share and post huge growth. International sales are just beginning and an oral version of Zepbound (which has shown great results) should be available sometime in late 2026. That will help it continue to capture share.

Second, the fears of regulatory burdens have been lessened. In fact, a recent deal with the administration to lower prices includes Medicare and Medicaid coverage, hugely expanding the market.

There’s even an AI angle here, with Lilly partnering with Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) to deploy the largest “AI factory” to be used on large-scale biomedical models. It also has deals with Google (GOOG, GOOGL) and OpenAI (OPENAI), and even has an AI lab smaller firms can use (for a fee, of course).

All told, analysts see growth slowing some from the current breakneck pace. But earnings should still rise 35% in 2026 (likely conservative), with much more beyond that.

After a long rest period, LLY has shown persistent strength in recent weeks, making it look to us like a liquid leader. That means it should be a magnet for growth-oriented institutional money.

Recommended Action: Buy LLY.

Enghouse Systems Ltd.

By Philip MacKeller, Editor of Contra the Heard

Enghouse Systems Ltd. (EGHSF, ENGH:CA) has had a rough few years and is well below the highs set during the peak of the pandemic in 2020. But as contrarian investors, we have taken a stake in this enterprise and are anticipating a recovery in the years ahead.

While we await the turnaround, we are being rewarded with dividends. The yield sat around 6% in late 2025.

Enghouse is a vertically focused software solutions provider headquartered in Ontario. In the latest quarter, around 70% of sales came from its recurring software-as-a-service (SaaS) segment. Most of the remaining 30% was generated from one-off software licenses and providing professional services, while hardware contributed a minor 2.7% of the top line.

The company has two business divisions: the Interactive Management Group and the Asset Management Group. The former provides customers with services to facilitate remote work, improve client engagement, and manage internal communications. Meanwhile, the latter offers software solutions for infrastructure, transportation, and supply chain challenges, among others.

The corporate growth strategy is two-fold. First, it drives business through internal investment, deepening existing customer relations, and funding new software services via R&D. Second, it pursues acquisitions with a focus on small companies with recurring software revenue. Their M&A strategy nearly exclusively focuses on bolt-on acquisitions, which they can buy outright with cash and integrate quickly.

The business fundamentals are solid. Enghouse has grown its top line over multiple business cycles, the balance sheet is cash-rich with hardly any debt, and it has produced consistent operating and free cash flows in each of the past 10 years. Insider alignment is strong, with executives and directors owning over 20% of the shares outstanding.

Its valuation has faltered because revenues and net income have slipped in the past year, with macro challenges mounting, customers cutting spending, and problems emerging with the SaaS business model. ENGH is also considered “boring old tech” rather than hyperscaling AI. While the company has used AI to cut costs and drive efficiencies, it has not aggressively used AI to increase sales.

Risks and all, Enghouse Systems is well-positioned, cheap, and pays a generous dividend. These features suggest it may reward owners with dividends and capital appreciation in the year ahead, which is why it is my top income pick for 2026.

Recommended Action: Buy ENGH.CA.

Enterprise Products Partners LP

By John Gardner, Founder and Principal of Blackhawk Wealth Advisors’ Market Insights

Enterprise Products Partners LP (EPD) is my top income pick, representing the ideal combination of a premium yield and unparalleled distribution safety within the essential North American energy midstream sector.

EPD operates one of the most extensive and diversified networks of pipelines, storage, and processing facilities for natural gas, natural gas liquids (NGLs), and crude oil, making it the essential infrastructure for transporting the continent’s most critical resources. The investment thesis for EPD centers on its stability and highly defensive financial structure.

Exceptional Income Profile: EPD offered an attractive recent distribution yield of approximately 7.5%. Crucially, this is not a speculative yield. The company has delivered an impressive track record of 27 consecutive years of distribution growth, making it a time-tested income compounder through multiple economic and energy cycles (including the 2014 oil crash and the Covid pandemic).

Best-in-Class Coverage and Safety: The distribution is protected by one of the strongest coverage ratios in the industry. EPD’s Distributable Cash Flow (DCF) provides approximately 1.7x coverage of its payout. This massive margin of safety ensures the distribution is virtually unassailable and allows the company to retain billions of dollars annually to fund its capital expenditure program.

Superior Financial Discipline: EPD’s conservative financial management is evidenced by its rare A- credit rating, the highest credit quality in the major midstream sector. This rating reflects low leverage and superior capital allocation, distinguishing EPD as a blue-chip choice for income investors seeking safety and scale.

Defensive Cash Flows: EPD’s revenues are primarily fee-based, driven by the volume of hydrocarbons transported, not the volatile price of the commodities themselves. This toll-road business model provides highly predictable and stable cash flows, offering an inflation hedge and defense against commodity price fluctuations.

For investors seeking a highly reliable, high-cash-flow return in a world facing increasing energy demand, EPD is the premier choice for compounding income.

Recommended Action: Buy EPD.

Expand Energy Corp.

By Jim Woods, Editor of Investing Edge

Dean Martin’s classic song, “Baby It’s Cold Outside,” is one of my favorite winter tunes. When temps are cold, demand for heat (which means demand for natural gas) soars. Keeping up with that nat gas demand, and providing it at a very nice profit to shareholders, is Oklahoma City-based Expand Energy Corp. (EXE).

The 2025/2026 winter season is shaping up to be one of the coldest in decades, and that means people are going to be dealing with the reality of Old Man Winter’s wrath. I reside in sunny Southern California, but even here the winter mornings are chilly. And because I’m an admitted weather wimp, temps south of about 70 degrees prompt me to turn on my heater (yes, you can laugh now).

Expand is an independent natural gas producer in the US. The company is focused on developing and selling the supply of natural gas, oil, and natural gas liquids (LNG). Its operations are located in the shale reserves of Louisiana, Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Ohio - and include interests in approximately 8,000 gross natural gas and oil wells.

Expand was formed in 2024, but it’s by no means a newbie on the scene. In fact, the company was born out of the merger of Chesapeake Energy and Southwestern Energy, which made it the largest natural gas producer.

In terms of one of the key metrics I use when selecting stocks, earnings per share growth, EXE is cooking with gas. In Q3 2025, Expand reported incredible EPS growth of 506% versus the same quarter a year earlier! Yes, you read that correctly - a five-fold increase over the prior year’s quarter!

Part of that increase was in anticipation of cold weather, but that’s not the only reason for the spike. It’s also because of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and the demand for nat gas used to power huge data centers churning out those massive, large language models (LLMs). And while the future of the AI buildout in 2026 has been a hot topic of conversation, my opinion is that the money will keep pouring in - and that means more and more energy demand.

If you want to own a company with multiple factors driving demand for its core product - one that also delivers a healthy dividend yield that’s north of 2% - it may be time to expand your holdings with Expand Energy.

Recommended Action: Buy EXE.

Federal Agricultural Mortgage Corp.

By Doug Gerlach, Editor of SmallCap Informer

Federal Agricultural Mortgage Corp. (AGM), often referred to as Farmer Mac, is an exceptional opportunity for long-term focused investors due to its blend of Government Sponsored Enterprise (GSE) stability, high-quality fundamentals, and attractive valuation.

The company’s unique mission-driven model goes beyond providing essential liquidity to American agriculture to include support of capital investments in all manner of rural infrastructure. That underpins a highly resilient business model designed to generate competitive shareholder returns across market cycles.

The fundamental strength of Farmer Mac is evidenced by its consistent growth and expanding footprint. Over the last ten years, the company achieved an 11.3% annualized revenue growth rate and a 15.1% annualized EPS growth rate. Looking forward, growth is projected at a solid 11% annually for both revenue and EPS, capitalizing on diversification across its seven operating segments.

The Infrastructure Finance line of business - including Renewable Energy and Broadband Infrastructure - is a key catalyst, driving substantial portfolio growth. In Q3 2025, Infrastructure Finance volume increased by $600 million, with Renewable Energy and Broadband volumes doubling or more year-over-year.

Quality metrics reinforce the investment thesis. Farmer Mac maintains a deeply conservative financial profile, defined by strong capital levels, stringent underwriting, and efficient operations. The company ended Q3 2025 with $723 million in core capital above the statutory minimum requirement, ensuring stability. Its operating discipline resulted in a 68.8% pre-tax profit margin in 2024 and an efficiency ratio maintained at 27%.

Furthermore, adherence to rigorous credit standards results in lifetime charge-off losses on agricultural mortgage loans of only 0.12%, while average portfolio Loan-to-Value (LTV) remains low at 47%. For shareholders, Farmer Mac offers consistent returns, having increased its quarterly dividend for 14 consecutive years, with a recent indicated yield of 3.5%.

The combination of continued earnings momentum and disciplined capital management translates to a compelling valuation. At the recent price of around $171, the stock traded in our recommended buy zone. The attractive projected reward/risk ratio stood at 3.4-to-1, supporting a projected total annual return of 16.9%.

Recommended Action: Buy AGM.

GE Vernova Inc.

By John Gardner, Founder and Principal of Blackhawk Wealth Advisors’ Market Insights

GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) is my top growth pick because it’s capitalizing on the massive, unavoidable investment cycle in electrification and grid modernization - a trend that has accelerated dramatically due to the demands of the Artificial Intelligence (AI) data center boom.

GEV is the global industrial leader that manufactures the essential hardware - from gas turbines and nuclear technology to grid transformers and advanced software - required to build and manage the world’s nextgeneration power system. The core thesis is simple: every utility, government, and hyperscaler racing to power new AI complexes must go through GEV.

Indeed, the company sits at the junction of two irreversible trends: the need for reliable, high-capacity baseload power (which requires GEV’s gas and nuclear segments) and the need for a smarter, more resilient grid (which requires GEV’s electrification segment) to handle erratic power loads. The three compelling financial pillars supporting this growth stock pick are...

Massive, Multi-Year Visibility: GEV’s Power and Electrification segment has a tremendous, growing order backlog. It is now near $130 billion and projected to reach $200 billion by 2028. This staggering figure provides a secure revenue floor and visibility well into the end of the decade, protecting the company from short-term economic turbulence and ensuring long-cycle profitable execution.

Explosive Earnings Acceleration: Strong earnings have propelled this 2024 General Electric Co. spin-off. Solid future earnings growth prospects are appealing. Consensus estimates project 34% EPS growth in 2025, with that acceleration expected to jump to over 70% in 2026.

Solving the Grid Bottleneck: The primary constraint on data center buildout today is the grid connection itself. GEV’s equipment - particularly its large transformers and sophisticated GridOS software - solves this bottleneck, making GEV an indispensable partner to every utility and tech firm racing to get power on-line. The company is strategically positioned to benefit from the highest-priority capital spending in the global energy ecosystem.

I project that the market is still underestimating the scope and duration of this power infrastructure buildout. Investing in GEV today is a strategic purchase of the factory floor for the future global energy system.

Recommended Action: Buy GEV.

Haverty Furniture Cos.

By Tracey Ryniec, Senior Stock Strategist at Zacks Investment Research

Haverty Furniture Cos. (HVT) is a home furnishing retailer with over 129 showrooms in 17 states in the Southern and Midwest regions. Its products are in the middle to upper-middle price ranges. Haverty has suffered through the last three years. But the bottom could be in.

The housing market has been in a recession since the Federal Reserve first raised interest rates back in 2022. Higher mortgage rates have resulted in lower home sales. This, in turn, trickles down to the furniture retailers. Consumers tend to buy a new couch, and other new furniture, when they buy a home.

As for Haverty, it is a retail institution founded in 1885. It has only had seven CEOs in its 140-year history. It is also one of the top 100 furniture manufacturers in the US.

Earnings have declined for several years as sales have slowed. But in the third quarter of 2025, it had its first positive written and delivered comp-store sales in several years. Comparable sales were up 7.1% in the third quarter of 2025, with comp-store written business up 8%.

Yes, that is coming off terrible numbers in 2024, so the bar was low. But Haverty continues to expand in preparation for a rebound in the housing market. In 2025, it opened a third Houston location and is targeting five net new store openings in 2026, including one in the first quarter.

Analysts are expecting a business turnaround in 2026. The Zacks Consensus Estimate is looking for earnings of $2.14, up 83.3% from $1.17 in 2025. Haverty also has a pristine balance sheet. As of September 30, 2025, it had $137 million in cash and cash equivalents on hand. It also had no debt outstanding and credit availability of $80 million.

The company is committed to its shareholders. It has paid a cash dividend each year since 1935. That includes throughout World War II, the Great Recession, and the Covid pandemic. On November 7, 2025, it raised the quarterly dividend a penny to $0.33 from $0.32. That is an annual dividend of $1.32 per share, which was recently good for a yield over 5%.

Haverty is attractively priced even though the shares are off the 2025 lows. It has a PEG ratio of just 1.8. The company has accounted for tariff impacts, as they knew them, in their full-year 2025 guidance.

Home sales will rise again, and consumers will need that new sectional. I would make a bet on small-cap furniture retailers with great balance sheets like Haverty.

Recommended Action: Buy HVT.

Originally posted on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Arm Holdings: Expect More Pressure As The Market Is Losing Patience
data.description:
Arm Holdings plc (NASDAQ:ARM) is down by 21% over the past year, and this performance is not a fluke. It also sends a very strong message to investors of the potential downside risk ahead as the narrative of high and sustainable future profits fizzles out.

As a starting point, it is worth noting that for the most part of 2025 ARM was trailing the broader semiconductors sector, but this changed rather quickly during the last quarter of calendar 2025.

ARM's performance begun diverging from that of the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) around the time when the company reported its Q2 2026 results, and the gap has continued widening ever since.

On the surface, the Q2 report was well-received by investors, which makes the ARM's share price performance even more worrisome.

To make matters even worse, last year was a very unusual one when it comes to equities and semiconductor stocks in particular. Demand for semiconductors hit a record high, with sales jumping 21% year-on-year.

More broadly, valuations of equities have skyrocketed, with the cyclically adjusted p/e ratio now at levels last seen during the dot-com bubble.

In such periods, ARM stock was supposed to deliver outstanding returns, but it didn't. The stock's beta of almost 4 suggests that to compensate investors for market risk, ARM should have delivered returns of above 50% (based on the S&P 500 return of 19% for the past 12-month period).

All that is now raising serious questions regarding ARM's expected performance in 2026, even though the most recent quarter and the guidance provided surprised on the upside.

The Q2 Report In Detail

One of the key developments during the last earnings report was ARM's management near-term guidance. Revenue for Q3 is expected to be around $1.225bn and Non-GAAP operating expenses to come at roughly $720m. This results in Non-GAAP operating income and margin of $455m and 41% respectively.

To put these numbers into perspective, the mid-point of the provided guidance would result in year-on-year revenue growth of just slightly below 25%, which although impressive is also roughly within the company's historical average.

On the margin side, the largest item within the company's Non-GAAP adjustments is the stock-based compensation expense, which came at $265m during the last reported quarter. Assuming that this figure will remain constant, the GAAP margin for Q3 should come at around 20%. On a historical basis, this is still an improvement, but the large fluctuations of ARM's quarterly GAAP margins does not allow us to simply extrapolate this improvement into the future.

Actual results in Q2 of FY 2026 also came in above the previously provided guidance. The extract below compares the Q2 guidance provided during the Q1 report (the table on the left-hand side) to the actual results reported (on the right-hand side). The difference was not significant, but still both revenue and Non-GAAP operating income came above estimates.

One issue with the revenue figure, however, is the fact that ARM saw a significant step-up in revenue from related parties, which to a certain extent makes the current growth figure far less impressive.

Lastly, ARM saw significant improvement in its cash flow from operations, with the share of stock-based compensation expense within that figure now being significantly reduced. This is definitely a step in the right direction, although it is still very early to draw any conclusions regarding the company's heavy reliance on share-based compensation packages.

Overall, the second quarter of FY 2026 was relatively good with some minor drawbacks but nothing the release suggested that the ARM stock would face significant pressure in the coming months. That is why, it is important for investors to take a step-back from the quarterly releases and focus on the bigger picture for ARM.

What To Expect In 2026?

What recent months have shown is that ARM's quarterly results and the near-term guidance have a very low impact on the share price performance. When a company is trading at around 140 times earnings, then it becomes extremely hard for investors to properly assess what is currently priced-in. In such scenarios, a company could consistently beat the quarterly estimates, deliver strong near-term guidance and still underperform the market for years to come.

Even on a Non-GAAP basis, ARM still trades at ridiculously high multiples which are hard to justify even at quarterly revenue growth rates of mid-20%. Not to mention the growing concerns of circular deals within the cloud space - a risk to which ARM would not be immune.

Another issue for ARM is the company's high exposure to the highly cyclical global smartphone market. As opposed to investments in data centers, smartphone sales more sensitive to changes in the business cycle in consumer confidence. In the U.S., for example, the consumer confidence index has collapsed in recent months and this could have grave consequences for the global smartphone market in 2026. As a matter of fact, this is one of the main reasons why the ARM stock was recently downgraded by Bank of America (BAC).

Both in the consumer electronics and the data center space, ARM's roadmap relies heavily on its Arm Neoverse Compute Subsystems (CSS). During the last earnings call, ARM's management stressed the importance of Neoverse CSS in both areas.

Customers are increasingly deploying Arm Neoverse CPUs alongside their AI accelerators to orchestrate massive clusters, highlighting the versatility and scalability of our platform.

The addition of 5 new Stargate sites this quarter further expands visibility into future AI capacity and reinforces is Arm's central role in the hyperscale build-out. (...) During the quarter, we signed 3 new CSS licenses, 1 each in smartphone, tablets and data centers, bringing our total to 19 CSS licenses across 11 companies. We also expanded our collaboration with Samsung, which is leveraging CSS for its Exynos family of chipsets, driving up to 40% AI performance over previous non-CSS generation.

As a result, the top 4 Android phone vendors are now shipping CSS-powered devices.

Source: ARM Q2 2026 Earnings Transcript.

Based on the extract above, it is quite clear that phone vendors remain of crucial importance for ARM, and the addition of the 5 new Stargate sites help explain the increase in revenue from related parties that we saw previously. It is most likely related to sales to Softbank (SFTBY), which owns a significant share of ARM.

Softbank's involvement in the Stargate Project could also explain the recent selling pressure of ARM as its major shareholder borrows against its ownership of ARM in order to fulfill its funding commitment to OpenAI (OPENAI).

Investor Takeaway

After ARM's performance detached from that of the semiconductors sector in the late months of 2025, I expect this trend to continue in calendar year 2026. The quarterly results will be of far less importance as ARM trades at extremely high multiples. The macroeconomic landscape and consumer spending in particular will be the key driving factor of investors' sentiment towards the stock. Lastly, Softbank's commitments towards the Stargate Project could easily turn into a headwind for ARM, in spite of the anticipated sales tailwind stemming from the project.

data.title: DoorDash: Too Expensive To Buy, But Too Good To Turn Bearish On
data.description:
I have been investing now for about 18 years. And for 13 of those years, I have been doing it professionally. I have always been a value investor with a contrarian streak. And one thing I will say is that while I firmly believe that value investing is the best way to make money in the market, there are two different big risks that value investors face. The first is the value trap. This is the kind of opportunity that looks great on paper. Because you are misunderstanding industry conditions, or you don't have as firm a grasp on the health of the company as you should, your assessment of it proves to be wrong. And the second would be the high-cost growth plays out there. There's always the risk of betting on a bigger fool. And unfortunately, while it is better to miss out on the downside by being overly conservative, it doesn't change the fact that missing out on the upside is still painful nonetheless.

In my opinion, one company that embodies this second category is DoorDash, Inc. (DASH). Back in July 2024, I decided to rate the company a "Hold." I acknowledged that the business was experiencing significant growth. But honestly, the trading multiples were too lofty for my liking. Since then, management has delivered in a big way. Revenue and cash flows have exploded higher, while the firm has finally turned profitable. The business continues to make interesting innovations as well. And as a result, shareholders have been well rewarded, with the stock rising 104.5% while the S&P 500 is up only 22.8%. From a long-term operational standpoint, I would argue that the business will do quite well. But honestly, it's more expensive now than it was back then from a valuation standpoint. At the risk of making the same mistake again, I believe that the high trading multiple that it is going for is difficult to justify. And as a result, I believe that upgrading it would be risky at this moment.

A Great Trip

Odds are, you know about DoorDash and what it does. But if you don't, the company essentially operates in the food delivery market. It has expanded over the years, including through its DoorDash Marketplace and Wolt Marketplace. It also has its Commerce Platform. The most recent big move the company made was to expand further, acquiring Deliveroo in a deal valued at £2.8 billion. This happens to be an online food delivery company that was founded in 2013 in London and that has since grown to have operations not only in the UK, but throughout Ireland, parts of mainland Europe, Asia, and the Middle East.

The bottom line here is that DoorDash has become a global food delivery, commerce, and logistics business. And the rapid growth that it has achieved in recent years has awarded it with a market capitalization of $98.32 billion. According to management, the company, which includes its ownership of Deliveroo, has over 50 million monthly active users worldwide. And as time goes on, we should certainly expect it to grow further.

It would be helpful to quantify the growth story that the company has experienced. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue shot up from $6.58 billion to $10.72 billion. Profitability has also improved drastically. The firm went from a net loss of $1.37 billion to a net profit of $123 million. But that's not all. Operating cash flow ballooned from $367 million to $2.13 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $294 million to $2 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $361 million to $1.90 billion.

The great thing about the company is that management provides vital financial metrics that help us understand this growth trajectory. For instance, in 2022, the company processed 1.74 billion orders on its platforms. That's a mind-boggling number. But that's small compared to what the company would ultimately go on to achieve. In 2024, orders came in at 2.58 billion. This is a testament to the continued growth of the business and the fact that so many individuals, myself included, find value in it. Not to evangelize, but I use the platform two or three times a week. I know it's more expensive than cooking at home or picking it up myself. But for somebody who works 80 or more hours a week, sometimes just hitting a couple of buttons on your laptop and waiting for the food to show up is worth it.

This is not the only growth metric that the company has demonstrated. Another one is Marketplace GOV. Management defines this as the total dollar value of orders completed on its marketplaces. This includes tips, taxes, customer fees, and even membership fees associated with DashPass and Wolt+. However, it does not include the dollar value of orders, taxes and tips, or fees that are charged to merchants for orders fulfilled on its Commerce Platform. This is something that should be talked about briefly. In essence, the Commerce Platform is a set of services that the company provides merchants that include white-label delivery fulfillment services, as well as other functionality that helps to build branded mobile apps, to enable tableside ordering, pay activities, and the like. So everything outside of this is included.

*There is no data for DashPass & Wolt+ Subscribers for Q3 of 2025.

Order growth and Marketplace GOV growth are only possible when the number of users that you have is growing also. And sure enough, management has data on that as well. The firm ended 2024 with 42 million monthly active users. That's up considerably from the 32 million that it had two years before that. As time goes on, this monthly active user count leads to a larger number of subscription customers as well. Included in these numbers would be 22 million DashPass and Wolt+ members. That's up from the 15 million reported back in 2022. The great thing about this kind of growth is that it translates to a larger base of consistent, reliable revenue for the company. Marketplace GOV, meanwhile, expanded from $53.41 billion to $80.23 billion.

We don't really know the impact this revenue has on the company. And that's because this is a global enterprise, especially because Wolt+ operates in several different countries with different pricing for each of them. But today, about 86.2% of DoorDash’s revenue comes from the US. And if we apply that to the DashPass numbers, that would be about 19 million subscription-paying members each year. The company charges $9.99 per month, or $96 annually. Assuming a split where 30% of users are paying monthly and the remaining 70% are on the annual plan, that would mean about $1.96 billion just from DashPass in 2024.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, investors do have data covering the first nine months. According to management, revenue amounted to $9.76 billion. That's up 24.4% over the $7.85 billion reported a year earlier. We don't have a detailed breakdown of some of the data. For instance, management did not provide a number of DashPass and Wolt+ subscribers. But we do know that, including the recently acquired Deliveroo, the company now has over 50 million monthly active users.

The overall growth that the company has achieved has permitted it to grow order count and Marketplace GOV. In the first nine months of 2025, the company achieved 2.27 billion orders. That happens to be 19.5% above the 1.90 billion reported a year earlier. Year over year, Marketplace GOV expanded from $58.95 billion to $72.36 billion. That's an increase of 22.7%. And with this growth has come additional economies of scale that have permitted the company to grow its bottom line. The net profit the business achieved in the first nine months of 2025 was $722 million. That compares to the $18 million loss reported previously. Operating cash flow jumped from $1.61 billion to $2.01 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, the rise was even more impressive, from $1.41 billion to $2.18 billion. And finally, EBITDA grew from $1.33 billion to $2 billion.

Management has gotten really innovative when it comes to growth opportunities. For instance, on December 17th, the company announced that it had partnered up with ChatGPT parent OpenAI to facilitate grocery shopping options directly through ChatGPT. In essence, there is a new DoorDash app within the chatbot that will enable customers to turn recipes into grocery orders that can then be delivered from local stores, sometimes in as little as an hour. This alone is a fascinating innovation that deserves attention. And it benefits from the roughly 800 million users that ChatGPT boasts. Of course, this feature will only be applicable in markets in which DoorDash operates. But already, the company has global reach, as I detailed.

Not all the company’s efforts are focused on high-tech things. For instance, in November of last year, management announced that it had partnered up with Family Dollar to offer convenient on-demand delivery from roughly 7,000 of the company's stores. And perhaps even more interesting is its highest tech venture yet, which involves its partnership with Waymo to launch an autonomous delivery service. For context, Waymo is owned by Google parent Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL). And it is my view that Waymo is very likely to win the autonomous vehicle race. Through this partnership, there would be an autonomous delivery service launched in Metro Phoenix that will involve a limited time $10 Waymo promotion for DashPass members in Los Angeles, San Francisco, and Phoenix. As autonomous driving becomes mainstream, which is something that will take many years to occur, companies like DoorDash stand to benefit. Autonomous vehicles will be cheaper than paying individuals to do the work. And it will allow quicker and easier expansion into new markets if done appropriately.

My only problem with DoorDash is that shares are very expensive right now. If we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2025, we are probably looking at adjusted operating cash flow of $3.08 billion. Management also said that, for the final quarter of 2025, EBITDA will be somewhere between $710 million and $810 million. At the midpoint, this would mean EBITDA for the year of about $2.76 billion. Using these estimates and also using the historical results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see how expensive the stock is in the chart above. Yes, I know this is a high-quality company. But these multiples are difficult to justify.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that while DoorDash is a great company with a bright future, the stock price is too expensive to get excited over. Even if we assume growth will continue at this rate, we are looking at multiples that would typically be associated with a bearish assessment. Recognizing that the market might still disagree with me moving forward, I cannot in good conscience rate the company any higher than a "Hold" right now.

data.title: Broadstone Net Lease: Buy This High Yield Overlooked By The Market
data.description:
Now remains a good time to be an income investor, and this includes a number of REITs with yields over 6%. At this yield threshold, investors are getting 6x the dividend yield of the S&P 500 (SPY). At this much of an income spread, I’m more content with getting a return of capital from income stocks rather than a return on capital on growth stocks at speculative valuations.

This brings me to Broadstone Net Lease, Inc. (BNL), which I last covered back in June 2025, highlighting its strategic shift toward industrial assets and attractive valuation. At the current price of $18.23, BNL trades at a still attractive 12x forward P/FFO and a 6.4% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit BNL including recent business results, and discuss why it remains a good income stock to own for total returns, so let’s get started!

Why BNL?

Broadstone Net Lease is a net lease REIT that focuses on mission-critical industrial properties, which represent 61% of portfolio ABR (annual base rent), and retail properties. It has a long weighted average lease term of 9.5 years, which is comparable with that of net lease peers like Realty Income Corp. (O) and W.P. Carey (WPC). It has 759 properties in total that’s diversified across 204 tenants.

In recent years, BNL has been shifting away from medical clinic exposure, thereby reducing complexity risks associated with healthcare properties and tenants. At present, BNL only has 15 clinic properties remaining, representing just 2.4% of portfolio ABR. As shown below, Industrial and Retail have grown significantly as part of the portfolio mix to 92%, while Healthcare and Office properties have shrunk to just 8% since 2023.

BNL stands out as having delivered a strong total return for shareholders, and yet trading at a discount to peers. Between January 1, 2023 and November 25, 2025, BNL has delivered a 33% total return while trading at a valuation lower than most peers, as shown below.

BNL’s strong total returns stems from an active portfolio management strategy, in which 97% of tenants provide financial reporting. Less than 5% of ABR comes from any single tenant, and the average potential problem tenant spends 1.5 years on the watchlist before a material credit event. This gives management ample time to work with tenants and resolve issues before they come to the foreground.

BNL demonstrated solid results in Q3 2025, with AFFO per share rising by 5.7% over the prior year period. This was driven by rent escalators, re-leasing of properties, and by additional properties in the portfolio. BNL also achieved 100% rent collection after having resolved previously challenged tenant situations involving At Home and Claire’s, with all leases being assumed and no bad debt incurred.

Management is also finding attractive investment opportunities, having invested $204 million during the quarter on accretive acquisitions and development projects. In the first 10 months of the year, BNL invested a total of $553 million, including $353 million in new property acquisitions and $150 million in build-to-suit developments. These portfolio additions came with an attractive weighted average cash cap rate of 7.1% and a straight-line yield of 8.2%. This was led by build-to-suit properties, which carry higher returns.

Looking ahead to Q4 results and beyond, management is guiding for full-year AFFO per share growth of 4.5% at the midpoint of the range. This is supported by a clear line of sight as it relates to portfolio health and investment activity. A key differentiator for BNL is its build-to-suit program, since they come with tenant guarantees and long-term contracts. This includes a 500+ acre site in Pennsylvania with significant industrial demand and access to nearby electric utility infrastructure. These factors help to ensure the long-term viability of this asset.

Over the medium term, BNL is set to benefit from secular trends of e-commerce growth and the related demand for logistics and distribution facilities. At the same time, the reshoring of manufacturing activity to the U.S. as a result of tariffs provides another boost to the industrial space. According to the U.S. Department of Commerce, e-commerce sales grew by 5.1% last year, outpacing total retail sales growth of 4.1%, and now makes up over 16% of total retail sales in the U.S. As shown below, this upward trend is likely to continue for the foreseeable future.

Importantly, BNL carries a strong balance sheet with a BBB credit rating from S&P. This is supported by a safe net debt to EBITDAre ratio of 5.4x, a fixed charge coverage ratio of 3.2x and $1.0 billion in total liquidity. This further supports the 6.4% dividend yield, which is well-covered by a 78% payout ratio. BNL has paid an uninterrupted and growing dividend since inception in 2020.

I continue to find value in BNL at the current price of $18.23 with a forward P/FFO ratio of 12.2, sitting slightly below its historical P/FFO of 12.3, as shown below. BNL also trades below the 13.8x and 16.7x P/FFOs of peers Realty Income Corp. and W.P. Carey.

At the current valuation, I believe the market isn’t giving BNL sufficient credit for its significant portfolio transition in recent years. This has resulted in a more focused portfolio that’s set to benefit from secular trends in demand for industrial real estate.

Plus, BNL has different avenues for growth, including its robust build-to-suit pipeline with attractive investment yields. Analysts estimate around 5% FFO/share growth annually over the next two years. This, combined with the 6.4% dividend yield, could lead to total returns in the low-teens from this durable income stock.

Risks to BNL include execution risk as it relates to its build-to-suit pipeline. This includes potential for construction delays and cost overruns, which could lower the initial investment yields. Unforeseen credit issues with tenants could result in pressure on rent growth. Plus, cyclicality in industrial markets due to economic volatility could lead to lower tenant demand and rent pressures.

Investor Takeaway

Broadstone Net Lease remains a great income pick for investors who prioritize reliable cash flow and reasonable growth over speculatively valued growth stocks. It has materially improved its portfolio focus by concentrating on industrial and retail assets, and exiting most healthcare exposure.

Lastly, BNL has disciplined balance sheet management and an active build-to-suit strategy that drives value. BNL offers an attractive risk-reward setup with AFFO growing in the mid-single digits, a well-covered 6.4% dividend, and shares trading at a discount to higher-profile net lease peers. For these reasons, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on the stock.

data.title: Stellantis Ditches Plug-In Hybrid Models, Tech That Struggled With U.S. Buyers
data.description:
Stellantis N.V.'s (STLA) decision to stop building Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles, or PHEVs, like the Jeep Wrangler 4xe and a handful of other PHEVs may surprise a few consumers, though not many who are familiar with U.S. auto industry dynamics.

A variety of factors is forcing Stellantis and other automakers to refocus their electrification strategies in light of broad consumer resistance to full battery electric vehicles (BEVs), radically altered federal regulatory policies and acceptance by automakers that the path to wide BEV acceptance in the U.S. will be slower and less predictable than anticipated.

Stellantis’ disclosure early this month of the Wrangler model discontinuation affects another Jeep with PHEV capability, Grand Cherokee 4xe, the Chrysler Pacifica plug-in hybrid minivan and the Alfa Romeo Tonale plug-in. Earlier, Stellantis cancelled the Jeep Gladiator 4xe pickup truck. The Dodge Hornet plug-in imported from Italy was canceled due to tariffs, according to the Automotive News.

Bust with buyers

PHEV models, usually costing a premium over gasoline models, have generally not sold well. Research suggests that owners often don’t plug them in and simply use them as they would gasoline models.

In light of broad confusion about what PHEV technology is, a short explanation: the system employs a battery, which must be recharged, and one or more electric motors to provide relatively short distances of electric-only travel, usually less than one hundred miles; a PHEV also is equipped with a gasoline motor that kicks in to drive the wheels when the battery is too low on charge to move the vehicle. General Motors Co.’s (GM) Chevy Volt, no longer manufactured, is a leading example.

Because BEVs such as those built by Tesla (TSLA) and other automakers require a large battery, need long charging times and have other problems, such as limited range and availability of charging stations (not to mention occasional overheating episodes), PHEV had been offered as a transitional technology until batteries became smaller, lighter, cheaper and more convenient to charge.

The gas-electric hybrid (HEV), a system pioneered by Toyota Motor Corp. (TM), features two power systems designed to operate in tandem for the purpose of improving fuel economy. Several automakers now offer HEVs. The latest of version of the Toyota Camry hybrid is rated at a combined 51 mpg, roughly 25% better than the Camry hybrid sold ten years ago and twice that of a gasoline version sold in 2016.

Stellantis was among the global automakers that chose to pursue a relatively limited electrification program of BEVs and PHEVs compared with General Motors (GM) and Ford Motor Co. (F); the company does not offer an HEV. The automaker, in light of regulatory changes, has cancelled its plan to build a battery version of its Ram full-size pickup truck, opting instead for an extended range electric version (EREV).

EREV technology employs a smaller battery than that found in a BEV and also features a plug and an onboard gasoline engine. In contrast with PHEVs, the EREV motor’s sole purpose is to recharge the battery when needed. Stellantis’s 1500 Ramcharger EREV, with nearly 700 miles of range, is due this year, as is a Jeep Wagoneer EREV.

Back to gas

All of which places Stellantis in an awkward position from a product standpoint, having to modify its electrification strategy in the middle of model renewal cycles. On the plus side, Stellantis isn’t facing massive write-offs due to investing too much too early on BEVs – as is the case at GM and Ford.

Another plus for Stellantis are the latest federal fuel efficiency rules revised by the Trump administration, which no long penalize automakers for producing gas-guzzling internal combustion engines. Accordingly, the automaker said in October it plans to invest $10 billion in U.S. manufacturing operations, focusing on Jeep and Dodge models. A new Dodge V8 muscle car has been mentioned as a possibility.

In perhaps a sign of the U.S. and Canadian auto market’s nostalgic affection for gasoline-powered models, Stellantis’s Dodge Charger Sixpack was named “Car of the Year” on Wednesday morning at an Automotive Press Association meeting during the Detroit Auto Show. The 550-horsepower Sixpack is equipped with a 3.0L twin turbo inline-six engine.

Stellantis brought Charger Sixpack to market after sales of an electric Dodge Daytona failed to catch on.

2025 was a difficult year for Stellantis, with revenue down 13% in the first half and a $2.7 billion loss explained mostly by write-offs taken due to a bloated vehicle inventory that needed to be cleared. By the third quarter, under the leadership of new CEO Antonio Filosa, operations were beginning to stabilize. The fourth quarter saw U.S. sales on a unit basis rise 4% YOY.

The company refers to 2025 as a “transition” year.

Could 2026 bring great performance for Stellantis, at least on a comparative basis? The shares peaked in March of 2024 touching $30. With two-thirds of its market capitalization lopped off at current prices, the stock could be a value play. The .77 dividend rate translates to a current 7.66% yield, assuming the dividend doesn’t get cut to help fund the investment in North America. Using Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating methodology, the shares rate a Dividend Safety score of F.

I remain cautious on Stellantis, though I like the direction of the company and the new leadership. Until investors see results from the new electrification strategy and how the manufacturing investments take hold the stock remains a Hold. Anyone with a high risk tolerance might want to begin building a position at the current share price, gambling that the dividend holds or is only slightly reduced.

data.title: Innovative Industrial: Why I Am Downgrading It
data.description:
I reiterated my Strong Buy on Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc. (IIPR) in December, and it is slightly lower now, but I am downgrading it to Hold. I continue to view it as a better option than many cannabis stocks, but I am concerned about the cannabis sector and REITs generally. In this piece, I explain why my views are less optimistic for IIPR.

Why I Like IIPR

As I stated in that last piece, IIPR seems very cheap. The stock trades below tangible book value (0.74X), and it has just a small amount of debt. The enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA for 2026 is just 8.9X, and the price to AFFO per share that I project ($7.19) is just 7X.

While I know that many are concerned about the move into partnership with IQHQ, I like that the company, which has employees that came from the life sciences REIT industry, has moved in this direction in order to diversify. When the management team entered the cannabis industry a decade ago, they did not know much about it. I credit them for doing a good job of learning! If 280E taxation remains in place, the cannabis industry will continue to struggle. The management team is filled with sharp people who know the life sciences REIT industry well. IIPR has invested in the preferred equity and the debt of IQHQ and not the common stock.

So, IIPR is cheap and well run, and I like it. I had a Strong Buy in August, when it was 20% of my model portfolio at 420 Investor, and the stock was a bit lower (near $47). I had initially rated it a Buy in December 2024 after the stock had crashed, happy that it was finally a Buy at $72.

What Concerns Me

At the core of the challenges IIPR faces is the financial health of its tenants. I have no near-term concerns about one of their tenants, Green Thumb Industries (GTBIF), which has low debt, but it has been just 9% of its rental income in the first three quarters of 2025. GTI is the second-largest tenant, behind Ascend Wellness, at 12% of the rent. Curaleaf (CURLF) was at 9%, while Trulieve (TCNNF) and Cannabist (CBSTF) were at 8%. I do have concerns about the other four MSO tenants, especially Curaleaf and Cannabist. If 280E taxation goes away (with cannabis getting rescheduled to Schedule III), it will be very good for the financials of these tenants.

I characterized the amount of debt as small, but the debt matures in May 2026. The senior notes were sold in 2021. When the company files its 10-K and has a conference call for its Q4 (in February, presumably), it may address this perhaps, though it could wait until the Q1 call in May, and it seems very likely that the interest-rate could rise from 5.5%. The company did repurchase some of the debt in February 2025, but it reported $291 million still outstanding as of 9/30. I assume that the rate will be going up, or the company would have already refinanced it. The company could also get a larger debt deal done, which would make the stock riskier in my view.

The current dividend yield is way too high at 15.2%. The $1.90 quarterly dividend exceeds the current adjusted funds from operations. I believe that the company should cut the dividend, but there has been no sign that it will do so. Perhaps as part of the debt refinancing, this will take place. I think that a dividend cut would be good for the stock in the long-run, but many investors might sell the stock.

Another concern of mine is that the company has been issuing preferred stock to repurchase common stock. I had previously mentioned this, but was glad to see the preferred stock holding. This is no longer the case, as the preferred stock, the Innovative Industrial Properties 9% Cumulative Preferred (IIPR.PR.A) has dropped more than 5% since I wrote about IIPR in December. It last traded at $22.74, which is below the $25.00 redemption price but above the low in April 2025 of $22.00. The volume has soared, and I think that IIPR investors should pay attention to this.

The cheap stock has gotten beat up for good reasons that include weak tenants (with many missed payments), maturing debt and a dividend that may be cut. The high-volume sell-off of the preferred stock is worrying me.

A Look At The Chart

IIPR posted a multi-year low in late November near $45. It is currently 12% higher. Here is the one-year chart:

As I pointed out above, the volume on the sinking preferred stock has been high recently. The volume on the common stock picked up in late December, when cannabis stocks were soaring on the potential rescheduling news, but it has declined in January.

IIPR is up 5.3% in 2026 so far, while the New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index is unchanged at 6.59. The AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS) is up 1.9%, closing at $4.81 on 1/13. The IIPR Preferred Stock has dropped 6.0%.

Taking a look at the action over the past five years, IIPR has outpaced MSOS:

Conclusion

While I have downgraded IIPR from Strong Buy to Hold, I still hold an 11.4% position in my model portfolio at 420 Investor, which is the same amount approximately as it was when I had it as Strong Buy. The model portfolio has 17% cash, which is below the maximum permitted of 20%, so I could trim more. The reality, though, is that for investors who want to be in the cannabis sector, IIPR remains a pretty good option.

For those who want to be in REITs, I have expressed a lot of concern about REITs, as I believe that they have far outpaced fixed-income, which is under pressure. I last wrote about this when I initiated a REIT ETF with a Sell rating and explaining why investors should be cautious.

While I don't cover the IIPR preferred stock (or any preferred stock), it looks cheap to me, and it might be a good substitute for holders of IIPR common. It is certainly safer than the common stock.
